title: Listening - Part 4: Digital Nomad Careers
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five individuals who left traditional office-based roles to work remotely while traveling

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been a project manager in a high-rise for a decade. While the paycheck was substantial, [21]the sheer monotony of the daily commute and the predictable office routine began to feel like a cage[/21]. I needed to see the world before I got too old. I transitioned to freelance consulting, and now I move cities every three months. It’s liberating, but there’s a catch. [26]The difficulty of maintaining a consistent sleep schedule across constantly changing time zones has been far more exhausting than I anticipated[/26]. Sometimes I’m on a Zoom call at 3 AM, wondering where I actually am. || Speaker 2: My friends were shocked when I quit my stable teaching job to become a remote copywriter. [22]I was desperate to escape the rigid, hierarchical structure of the school system[/22] where I had zero autonomy. Now, I work from beaches in Bali or cafes in Lisbon. The freedom is intoxicating. However, it’s not all sunsets and cocktails. [27]I’ve found that the lack of face-to-face interaction with colleagues often leads to a deep sense of professional isolation[/27]. You don’t realize how much you value a quick chat by the coffee machine until your only coworkers are emojis in a Slack channel. || Speaker 3: I worked in a busy architectural firm, and [23]the environmental impact of the massive commercial projects we were building started to weigh heavily on my conscience[/23]. I wanted to focus on sustainable, small-scale design while traveling through South America. It’s been an eye-opener, but the logistics are a nightmare. [28]The most frustrating aspect is the unreliable infrastructure, particularly the sudden internet outages during crucial client meetings[/28]. You can’t exactly be a 'digital' nomad when the Wi-Fi disappears for three days in the middle of a deadline. || Speaker 4: I was a corporate lawyer in New York, and [24]I realized I was missing out on my children’s lives because I was chained to my desk until midnight[/24]. I moved us all to a coastal town in Portugal to work remotely. Now I see them every morning, which is priceless. But the locals don't always see it that way. [29]The most challenging part has been the subtle resentment from the local community regarding the rise in local property prices[/29]. You feel like an outsider who is inadvertently making life harder for the people who actually live here permanently. || Speaker 5: I was a software developer for a tech giant, but [25]I felt like a tiny, insignificant cog in a massive machine that didn't care about my input[/25]. I wanted to build my own apps while exploring different cultures. I’ve learned so much about global markets, but the move hasn't been great for my mental health. [30]I’ve struggled to separate my work life from my personal life, often finding myself answering emails in bed at midnight[/30]. Without a physical office to leave, the working day never truly ends. I’m constantly 'on,' and that’s a direct recipe for burnout.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21 – 25, choose from the list (A – H) why each speaker decided to become a digital nomad.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 felt the 'predictable office routine' and commute were like a 'cage'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to escape the 'rigid, hierarchical structure' and lack of autonomy.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 had concerns about the 'environmental impact' of her firm's projects.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to stop 'missing out on my children’s lives' due to long hours.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 felt like an 'insignificant cog' in a machine that didn't value his input.

options.A: to save money on living costs
options.B: to spend more time with family
options.E: to feel more valued in their role
options.G: to align their work with personal values
options.H: to follow a partner's career move

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26 – 30, choose from the list (A – H) what each speaker finds most difficult about their new lifestyle.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 struggles with maintaining a sleep schedule across 'changing time zones'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions a 'deep sense of professional isolation' without face-to-face contact.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 cites 'unreliable infrastructure' and internet outages as the main frustration.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 notes 'subtle resentment' from locals due to rising property prices.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 finds it hard to 'separate work life from personal life', leading to overworking.

options.E: the social impact on the local area
options.G: the lack of a permanent home base
